findings.title: No script generates the varying-initial-step-size experiment (Figs 10–11)
findings.quote:
In the final experiment, we examine the effect of the initial
value α1 > 0 in the step-size schedule. For SGD, we set αk = α1k−1/2, while for reg-SGD we
fix λk = k−0.111 and use the step-size schedule αk = α1k−0.667. We report both the pathwise
optimality gap and the pathwise squared error to the minimum-norm solution for SGD (Figure 10)
and reg-SGD (Figure 11) under varying initial step sizes α1 ∈ {0.01, 0.1, 1, 2}.
findings.claim: Appendix A.2 reports Figures 10 and 11 sweeping the initial step size α1 ∈ {0.01,0.1,1,2}; the three toy-example scripts (main_L2rates.m, main_asrates.m, main_comparison.m) sweep (p,q) and rates but none loops over α1 or reproduces Figs 10/11.
findings.concern: The α1-sweep figures cannot be reproduced from the provided code.
findings.resolution: Authors: please add the script that produces Figures 10–11 (the α1 sweep), or confirm it was a minor variant run off-repo.
findings.reason: The three toy-example scripts (main_L2rates.m, main_asrates.m, main_comparison.m) are the only .m files in Toy_example/ and none contains any α1 sweep; the paper text (line 1313–1315) confirms the α1 ∈ {0.01,0.1,1,2} experiment is described but no corresponding script exists.

findings.title: ODE example uses 63 observation points, paper states 64
findings.claim: The ODE inverse-problem script sets the number of observation points to K = 2^6-1 = 63.
findings.concern: Appendix A.3 states K = 64 equidistant observation points (s_k = k/K, k=1,…,K); the code uses 63, a one-off mismatch in the discretization size.
findings.resolution: Authors: confirm whether K=63 (interior nodes) or K=64 was used to produce Figs 12–13, and reconcile with the appendix.
findings.reason: ODE_example/main_ODE.m line 8 reads 'K = 2^6-1;' (= 63), confirmed at the exact cited lines, while paper_text.txt line 1338 explicitly states 'K = 64 equidistant observation points'.

findings.title: ODE regularization constant is 0.002 in code, 0.001 in paper
findings.claim: The ODE script uses regularization schedule lambda_k = 0.002 * k^{-1/3}.
findings.concern: Appendix A.3 states lambda_k = 0.001 k^{-1/3}; the code's prefactor (0.002) is twice the reported value.
findings.resolution: Authors: confirm the regularization prefactor used for Figs 12–13 (0.001 vs 0.002).
findings.reason: ODE_example/main_ODE.m line 114 reads 'lambdak = 0.002/k^p;' at the exact cited spot; paper_text.txt (grep context) confirms Appendix A.3 states 0.001 — a 2× discrepancy in the prefactor.

findings.title: Toy a.s.-rate script uses gradient-noise std 1, paper A.2 states 0.1
findings.claim: main_asrates.m perturbs the gradient with N(0,1) noise (std 1, covariance 1·Id).
findings.concern: Appendix A.2 states the toy example perturbs the gradient with covariance 0.1²·Id (std 0.1); the a.s.-rate script uses std 1 — 100× larger variance — and an SGD/reg-SGD step prefactor 0.05 rather than the stated 0.1.
findings.resolution: Authors: confirm the noise covariance and step-size prefactor used for Figure 7 (a.s. rates) vs the values quoted in Appendix A.2.
findings.reason: Toy_example/main_asrates.m line 68 reads 'noisygrad = 1*randn(n,1);' at the exact cited spot, while paper_text.txt line 1265 confirms Appendix A.2 states 'covariance 0.1² · Id' — confirming the 100× variance discrepancy.

findings.title: Toy L²-rate SGD step prefactor is 1, paper A.2 states 0.1
findings.quote:
% learning rate for SGD without reg
step_sizek_literature = 1./k^(1/2);
findings.claim: main_L2rates.m sets the plain-SGD step size to 1·k^{-1/2} (and reg-SGD step to 1·k^{-q}).
findings.concern: Appendix A.2 states αk = 0.1k−1/2 for SGD (and αk = 0.1k−q for reg-SGD); the L²-rate script omits the 0.1 prefactor (uses 1), so the simulated step sizes differ from the reported ones.
findings.resolution: Authors: confirm the step-size prefactor used for Figure 6 (0.1 vs 1).
findings.reason: Toy_example/main_L2rates.m lines 73–76 confirm reg-SGD uses '1/k^q' and SGD uses '1./k^(1/2)' — both with prefactor 1 — while paper_text.txt line 1265–1266 states both should use prefactor 0.1.

findings.title: Comparison script step law q·0.2·k^{-q} differs from paper's 0.2q·k^{-1/2}
findings.claim: The (p,q)-comparison script uses step size α_k = q·0.2·k^{-q} (decay exponent q, varying per configuration).
findings.concern: Appendix A.2 (Figs 8–9) writes the step size as αk = 0.2q·k−1/2 (a fixed k^{-1/2} decay); the code instead decays with each configuration's own q, so the step-size schedule used differs from the formula printed in the paper (the (p,q) value set itself matches).
findings.resolution: Authors: confirm the step-size exponent for the (p,q)-comparison experiment — fixed k^{-1/2} as written, or per-configuration k^{-q} as coded.
findings.reason: Toy_example/main_comparison.m lines 112–115 show 'step_sizek = q*0.2/k^q;' at the exact cited spot, while paper_text.txt line 1290 confirms Appendix A.2 states 'αk = 0.2qk^{-1/2}' (fixed k^{-1/2} exponent, not k^{-q}).

findings.title: ODE KL-basis coefficient is sqrt(2*pi) in code vs sqrt(2)/pi in paper
findings.claim: The KL basis used to (re)generate the ground-truth rhs x† scales each sine mode by sqrt(2*pi) ≈ 2.507.
findings.concern: Appendix A.3 defines x†(s) = Σ (√2/π) ξ_i sin(iπs) with coefficient √2/π ≈ 0.450; the code uses √(2π) ≈ 2.507, a ~5.6× different amplitude. Effect is limited because the actual run loads a saved m_ref.mat (regeneration at lines 37-38 is commented out), so it only matters if the reference is regenerated.
findings.resolution: Authors: confirm the basis normalization (√2/π vs √(2π)) and whether the saved m_ref.mat was produced with the paper's formula.
findings.reason: Opus re-check confirms Sonnet's lowered verdict against the cited file ODE_example/main_ODE.m. Line 24 reads 'V_basis(:,j) = sqrt(2*pi)*sin(j*pi*xx)'' (sqrt(2*pi) ~ 2.507) versus the paper's sqrt(2)/pi ~ 0.450 — a real ~5.6x amplitude mismatch, confirmed at the cited lines. However the discrepancy is latent: the regeneration block at lines 36-38 (m_ref = V_basis*sqrt(D)*randn(...); save('m_ref.mat',...)) is commented out, line 41 loads the pre-saved load('m_ref.mat','m_ref'), and m_ref.mat ships in the ODE_example directory. So in normal use the published Figs 12-13 use the saved reference and are unaffected unless a user manually re-enables regeneration. Severity stays low and confidence is lowered from medium to low to reflect the latent/no-impact nature.
findings.changed: confidence lowered from medium to low; severity unchanged (Opus confirms Sonnet's lowered)

findings.title: Default iteration counts far below the figure captions (10^3–10^5 vs 5·10^6–10^7)
findings.claim: Shipped defaults are final_iterate = 10^3 (Radon), 10^4 (toy L²/a.s., ODE), 10^5 (toy comparison).
findings.concern: The figure captions report runs of length N = 5·10^6 (Fig 5), 10^6 (Figs 6–7), and 10^7 (Figs 9–12); the shipped defaults are 10^3–10^5, so running the code as-is does not reproduce the published curves. The readme explicitly notes 'the user may select an increased final iterate', so this is an acknowledged convenience default rather than a hidden discrepancy.
findings.resolution: No action strictly required (acknowledged); optionally ship the exact N used per figure as a comment or config so the published plots are reproducible without guessing.
findings.reason: Radon/main_radon.m line 51 reads 'final_iterate = 10^3;' at the exact cited spot, confirming the default is 1000 versus the paper's figure-caption values of 5·10^6–10^7.
